coach_note:
Mid-May update. Chris ran 39:04 at Colchester 10K — a new PB and his first time under 39:30. He's a touch disappointed because he didn't dip under 39, but the execution issues he describes (the 500m interval surging cycle, the deceiving hill at 9km, conservative thinking in the final km) all suggest there's more in the tank. By his own admission, when he was running 3:45/km pace he 'felt really good' — that's a meaningful indicator for shorter race distances. The Harwich 5K on 19 May is now the focus, and a sub-19 attempt is firmly on the table.

We've recalibrated all upcoming pace targets to his new 39:00 10K fitness: 1K reps at 4:00-4:07/km (15K pace), 2K reps at 4:06-4:13/km (HM pace), 3K reps at 4:12-4:19/km (30K pace). Garmin structured workouts now use pace targets instead of HR % so the watch can guide him through reps properly. He's also added Southend Half Marathon on 14 June as a B-race — that fits neatly at the end of the block as a longer fitness check.

The broader story: 12 weeks ago we estimated 20:00 5K fitness post-illness. He's now sitting on 39:04 10K (≈19:00 5K equivalent) and the trend is still pointing up. CTL has climbed from 29 to 41 over the block. HRV is at all-time highs (multiple 80s and 90s readings this past week, peaking at 97 today). Resting HR has dropped to 32-35 range. He's adapting brilliantly. The next two weeks should be about precision execution rather than fitness gains — the fitness is there, he needs to race it cleanly.

Key reflection for Harwich 19 May: His own diagnosis of the Colchester pacing — chasing 500m splits and yo-yoing — is the right lesson. For a 5K, the plan should be simple. Lock onto 3:48/km from km 1 and ride it. No watch surging. Let it feel uncomfortable from km 2 onwards. The fitness is there.
goal_race: Harwich 5K Race 2 (19 May), Southend HM (14 Jun)

race_history.notes: First race back. Hard effort with headwind on the run-in. Pleased with result.
Windy conditions (gusts to 13m/s, 44% headwind). Good effort but conditions tough.
New PB. Executed poorly per Chris's own assessment — 500m splits caused surging/yo-yoing. Felt great at 3:45/km when surging. Deceiving hill at 9km cost time. GPS ran long. Sub-39 still attainable.

wellness_notes:
Wellness as of 13 May 2026 — Chris is in the strongest physical state of the block. Resting HR has dropped to 32-36 range (was 35-38 a month ago). HRV has reached new highs: 72, 85, 82, 63, 80, 69, 72, 85, 83, 97 over the past 10 days. Today's 97 is the highest of the block. CTL is 41.4, stable around 40-41 for the past 2 weeks — a healthy plateau after the climb from 29. ATL is 47.6, well-balanced (TSB around -6). Weight stable at 71.7kg. VO2max Garmin estimate at 62. Sleep variable as ever (scores 64-89, quality mostly 2-3) — his own admission that the past week's sleep, nutrition and travel hasn't been ideal is consistent with the lower sleep scores 5-8 May. Despite that, training and racing have been strong, which speaks to his underlying fitness reserve. 

Key watch items: (1) HRV at 97 today is unusually high — could indicate either excellent recovery or possible parasympathetic dominance. Worth monitoring whether the trend continues. (2) Resting HR 35 today is normal for him. (3) The disclaimers Chris gave about poor recent sleep/nutrition are worth noting but didn't visibly hurt the race result. (4) Plan around Southend HM 14 Jun: this is a step up in distance — he hasn't raced longer than 10K in this block. The week 9 long run of 16km and week 10 long run of 20km will help, but a proper HM-specific block would be ideal next.

ctl.interpretation: Healthy plateau after sharp build. Indicates training load is well-absorbed.

hrv_avg_7d.interpretation: Major improvement. From mid-50s baseline at start of block to consistent 70s-90s now. Today peaked at 97.

resting_hr_avg_7d.interpretation: Solidly in 32-36 range. Down from 35-38 baseline. Indicates strong cardiovascular adaptation.

sleep_score_avg_7d.interpretation: Inconsistent (range 64-89). Chris flagged recent sleep as poor — area to address.

vo2max_garmin.interpretation: Up from 58 at start of block.

upcoming_training_notes:
All upcoming structured workouts updated from HR-based (86-91% HR) to pace-based targets. Pace zones derived from his new 39:00 10K fitness per the standard percentage table: 1K reps at 15K pace (4:00-4:07/km), 2K reps at HM pace (4:06-4:13/km), 3K reps at 30K pace (4:12-4:19/km).

Next race: Harwich 5K Race 2 on Tue 19 May. Pre-race week (this week): 8x1km Wed 13 May (PACE-BASED), 3x3km Fri 15 May (BENCHMARK, last hard session before race), easy Sat, long Sun. Race Tuesday.

New B-race added: Southend Half Marathon on 14 June (originally the long run day). The week 10 plan now reframes around this: keep the 3x3km benchmark Thu 11 Jun, replace the 20km long run with the Southend HM itself.

Pacing guidance for Harwich 5K (sub-19 attempt): Target 3:48/km (or 1:54 per 500m) but DO NOT chase splits within a lap. Lock onto effort by km 1, hold through km 2-3, push hard from km 4. Set watch to whole-km laps not 500m to avoid the surging cycle from Colchester.
pacing_lessons_from_colchester: Chris's own race report identified the key execution issues clearly: (1) 500m intervals caused him to surge whenever he saw a split slower than target, then over-correct. (2) The 'deceiving hill' at 9km broke his rhythm in the final km. (3) He went conservative at 8.5-9km when he could have pushed harder. (4) When running 3:45/km felt good in the surges — suggests genuine sub-19 5K fitness is present. Key takeaway: simpler pacing strategy for shorter races. Lock onto effort, not splits.

predictions.rationale: 39:04 10K (3:54/km avg) extrapolates to ~18:50-19:00 over 5K. Surging at 3:45/km felt good — that's sub-19 pace for him. With cleaner pacing (whole-km laps, no surging) and a non-windy day, sub-19 is achievable. Conservative bound accounts for residual fatigue from 10K race 9 days prior.
By Race 3 Chris will have 3 more weeks of training plus a recovery week. If Race 2 confirms sub-19 fitness, sub-18:30 is realistic. CTL should hold around 40-42.

39:00 10K extrapolates to ~1:25:30 HM via standard race conversion tables. Chris hasn't done HM-specific training in this block, so his pace will likely fade in the second half. But the aerobic base from Norwegian Singles work should hold up well at HM effort. Conservative range allows for the longer distance being relatively untrained territory.
